BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Increase in danger of wet avalanches in the course of the day.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
As the day progresses as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation there will be an appreciable increase in the danger of wet avalanches. Wet avalanches can in some places be released by a single winter sport participant. This applies on very steep shady slopes below approximately 2500 m, as well as on very steep sunny slopes at elevated altitudes. Natural avalanches are possible in the afternoon. Avalanches can be released in the weakly bonded old snow and reach large size in isolated cases.

Dry avalanches can additionally be released, even by small loads in isolated cases, especially on very steep west, north and east facing slopes above approximately 2500 m. Especially transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack are unfavourable. The avalanche prone locations are difficult to recognise.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
The surface of the snowpack will freeze to form a strong crust and will soften during the day. Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise to increasing and thorough wetting of the snowpack below approximately 3000 m.
Steep west, north and east facing slopes: Faceted weak layers exist in the bottom section of the old snowpack at elevated altitudes.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: Outgoing longwave radiation during the night will be good. The backcountry touring conditions in the morning at elevated altitudes are generally favourable. As the day progresses as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation there will be an increase in the danger of wet avalanches.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Increase in danger of wet avalanches in the course of the day.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
As the day progresses as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation there will be an appreciable increase in the danger of wet avalanches. Wet avalanches can in some places be released by a single winter sport participant. This applies on very steep shady slopes below approximately 2500 m, as well as on very steep sunny slopes at elevated altitudes. Natural avalanches are possible in the afternoon. Avalanches can be released in the weakly bonded old snow and reach large size in isolated cases.

Dry avalanches can additionally be released, even by small loads in isolated cases, especially on very steep west, north and east facing slopes above approximately 2500 m. Especially transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack are unfavourable. The avalanche prone locations are difficult to recognise.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
The surface of the snowpack will freeze to form a strong crust and will soften during the day. Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise to increasing and thorough wetting of the snowpack below approximately 3000 m.
Steep west, north and east facing slopes: Faceted weak layers exist in the bottom section of the old snowpack at elevated altitudes.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: Outgoing longwave radiation during the night will be good. The backcountry touring conditions in the morning at elevated altitudes are generally favourable. As the day progresses as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation there will be an increase in the danger of wet avalanches.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Weakly bonded old snow and wet snow represent the main danger.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
As a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation there will be an increase in the danger of wet avalanches. From origins in starting zones where no previous releases have taken place natural avalanches are possible, in particular medium-sized ones. This applies on shady slopes below approximately 2800 m, as well as on sunny slopes at elevated altitudes. On steep shady slopes avalanches can be released in the weakly bonded old snow and reach large size in isolated cases.

Individual avalanche prone locations for dry avalanches are to be found on very steep shady slopes above approximately 2400 m. Such avalanche prone locations are difficult to recognise. Especially transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack are unfavourable.

In addition the fresh wind slabs in high Alpine regions are capable of being triggered in isolated cases. Wind slabs are mostly small.

Backcountry tours and off-piste skiing should be started and concluded early.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
The surface of the snowpack will freeze to form a strong crust only at high altitudes and will already soften in the late morning. Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise to increasing and thorough wetting of the snowpack below approximately 2800 m. The high humditiy will give rise in the afternoon to a loss of strength within the snowpack also on steep shady slopes.

Steep west, north and east facing slopes above approximately 2400 m: Faceted weak layers exist in the bottom section of the old snowpack at elevated altitudes.

Below approximately 1800 m from a snow sport perspective, in most cases insufficient snow is lying.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment:
On Friday it will be sunny. The weather will be mild.
The backcountry touring conditions in the morning at elevated altitudes are generally favourable. As the day progresses as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation there will be an increase in the danger of wet avalanches.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Increase in danger of wet avalanches in the course of the day.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
As the day progresses as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation there will be an appreciable increase in the danger of wet avalanches. Wet avalanches can in some places be released by a single winter sport participant. This applies on very steep shady slopes below approximately 2500 m, as well as on very steep sunny slopes at elevated altitudes. Natural avalanches are possible in the afternoon. Avalanches can be released in the weakly bonded old snow and reach large size in isolated cases.

Dry avalanches can additionally be released, even by small loads in isolated cases, especially on very steep west, north and east facing slopes above approximately 2500 m. Especially transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack are unfavourable. The avalanche prone locations are difficult to recognise.
The no longer entirely fresh wind slabs can be released by a single winter sport participant in isolated cases in particular on very steep shady slopes in high Alpine regions.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
The surface of the snowpack will freeze to form a strong crust and will soften during the day. Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise to increasing and thorough wetting of the snowpack below approximately 3000 m.
Steep west, north and east facing slopes: Faceted weak layers exist in the bottom section of the old snowpack at elevated altitudes.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: Outgoing longwave radiation during the night will be good. The backcountry touring conditions in the morning at elevated altitudes are generally favourable. As the day progresses as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation there will be an increase in the danger of wet avalanches.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Increase in danger of wet avalanches in the course of the day.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
As the day progresses as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation there will be an appreciable increase in the danger of wet avalanches. Wet avalanches can in some places be released by a single winter sport participant. This applies on very steep shady slopes below approximately 2500 m, as well as on very steep sunny slopes at elevated altitudes. Natural avalanches are possible in the afternoon. Avalanches can be released in the weakly bonded old snow and reach large size in isolated cases.

Dry avalanches can additionally be released, even by small loads in isolated cases, especially on very steep west, north and east facing slopes above approximately 2500 m. Especially transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack are unfavourable. The avalanche prone locations are difficult to recognise.
The no longer entirely fresh wind slabs can be released by a single winter sport participant in isolated cases in particular on very steep shady slopes in high Alpine regions.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
The surface of the snowpack will freeze to form a strong crust and will soften during the day. Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise to increasing and thorough wetting of the snowpack below approximately 3000 m.
Steep west, north and east facing slopes: Faceted weak layers exist in the bottom section of the old snowpack at elevated altitudes.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: Outgoing longwave radiation during the night will be good. The backcountry touring conditions in the morning at elevated altitudes are generally favourable. As the day progresses as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation there will be an increase in the danger of wet avalanches.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: The danger of moist and wet avalanches will already increase in the late morning.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
The high temperatures in the late morning will give rise to a loss of strength within the snowpack on sunny slopes at high altitudes and in high Alpine regions. This situation will give rise to extreme and thorough wetting of the snowpack over a wide area in all aspects at low and intermediate altitudes. Naturally triggered avalanches and penetration of the old snow cover show the existence of a weak snowack.

In particular on very steep slopes more medium-sized and, in isolated cases, large gliding avalanches and moist snow slides are possible as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation.
High altitudes and the high Alpine regions: Avalanches can in isolated cases be released in the old snowpack and reach medium size on steep sunny slopes. This applies even in case of a single winter sport participant in some cases.

Backcountry tours should be concluded timely.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
Up to 60 cm of snow fell on Monday above approximately 2200 m. The new snow of Monday has settled to a large extent.

Below approximately 3000 m: The snowpack is moist and its surface has a melt-freeze crust. The surface of the snowpack will already soften in the late morning.

Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise as the day progresses to rapid moistening of the snowpack. This applies in particular on steep sunny slopes at high altitudes and in high Alpine regions, and in all aspects at low and intermediate altitudes.
Below approximately 2400 m only a little snow is lying on south facing slopes.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: The danger of moist and wet avalanches will already increase in the late morning.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
The high temperatures in the late morning will give rise to a loss of strength within the snowpack on sunny slopes at high altitudes and in high Alpine regions. This situation will give rise to extreme and thorough wetting of the snowpack over a wide area in all aspects at low and intermediate altitudes. Naturally triggered avalanches and penetration of the old snow cover show the existence of a weak snowack.

In particular on very steep slopes more medium-sized and, in isolated cases, large gliding avalanches and moist snow slides are possible as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation.
High altitudes and the high Alpine regions: Avalanches can in isolated cases be released in the old snowpack and reach medium size on steep sunny slopes. This applies even in case of a single winter sport participant in some cases.

Backcountry tours should be concluded timely.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
Up to 60 cm of snow fell on Monday above approximately 2200 m. The new snow of Monday has settled to a large extent.

Below approximately 3000 m: The snowpack is moist and its surface has a melt-freeze crust. The surface of the snowpack will already soften in the late morning.

Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise as the day progresses to rapid moistening of the snowpack. This applies in particular on steep sunny slopes at high altitudes and in high Alpine regions, and in all aspects at low and intermediate altitudes.
Below approximately 2400 m only a little snow is lying on south facing slopes.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: The danger of moist and wet avalanches will already increase in the late morning.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
The high temperatures in the late morning will give rise to a loss of strength within the snowpack on sunny slopes at high altitudes and in high Alpine regions. This situation will give rise to extreme and thorough wetting of the snowpack over a wide area in all aspects at low and intermediate altitudes. Naturally triggered avalanches and penetration of the old snow cover show the existence of a weak snowack.

In particular on very steep slopes and in steep rocky terrain more small and medium-sized gliding avalanches and moist snow slides are possible as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation.
High altitudes and the high Alpine regions: The near-surface layers of the snowpack can be released in isolated cases by individual winter sport participants.
Backcountry tours should be concluded timely.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
Over a wide area 40 to 60 cm of snow fell on Monday above approximately 2000 m. The new snow of Monday has settled to a large extent.

Below approximately 3000 m: The snowpack is moist and its surface has a melt-freeze crust. The surface of the snowpack will already soften in the late morning.

Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise as the day progresses to rapid moistening of the snowpack. This applies in particular on steep sunny slopes at high altitudes and in high Alpine regions, and in all aspects at low and intermediate altitudes.
Below approximately 2400 m only a little snow is lying on south facing slopes.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: The danger of moist and wet avalanches will already increase in the late morning.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
The high temperatures in the late morning will give rise to a loss of strength within the snowpack on sunny slopes at high altitudes and in high Alpine regions. This situation will give rise to extreme and thorough wetting of the snowpack over a wide area in all aspects at low and intermediate altitudes. Naturally triggered avalanches and penetration of the old snow cover show the existence of a weak snowack.

In particular on very steep slopes and in steep rocky terrain more small and medium-sized gliding avalanches and moist snow slides are possible as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation.
High altitudes and the high Alpine regions: The near-surface layers of the snowpack can be released in isolated cases by individual winter sport participants.
Backcountry tours should be concluded timely.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
Over a wide area 40 to 60 cm of snow fell on Monday above approximately 2000 m. The new snow of Monday has settled to a large extent.

Below approximately 3000 m: The snowpack is moist and its surface has a melt-freeze crust. The surface of the snowpack will already soften in the late morning.

Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise as the day progresses to rapid moistening of the snowpack. This applies in particular on steep sunny slopes at high altitudes and in high Alpine regions, and in all aspects at low and intermediate altitudes.
Below approximately 2400 m only a little snow is lying on south facing slopes.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Increase in danger of wet avalanches in the course of the day.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
As the day progresses as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation there will be an increase in the danger of wet avalanches. Wet avalanches can in some places be released by a single winter sport participant. This applies on very steep shady slopes below approximately 2500 m, as well as on very steep sunny slopes at elevated altitudes.

Individual avalanche prone locations for dry avalanches are to be found on very steep shady slopes above approximately 2500 m. Especially transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack are unfavourable. The avalanche prone locations are difficult to recognise.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
The surface of the snowpack has frozen to form a strong crust and will soften during the day. Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise to gradual softening of the snowpack below approximately 3000 m.
Steep west, north and east facing slopes: Faceted weak layers exist in the bottom section of the old snowpack at elevated altitudes.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: Outgoing longwave radiation during the night will be good. The backcountry touring conditions in the morning at elevated altitudes are generally favourable. As the day progresses as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation there will be an increase in the danger of wet avalanches.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Increase in danger of wet avalanches in the course of the day.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
As the day progresses as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation there will be an increase in the danger of wet avalanches. Wet avalanches can in some places be released by a single winter sport participant. This applies on very steep shady slopes below approximately 2500 m, as well as on very steep sunny slopes at elevated altitudes.

Individual avalanche prone locations for dry avalanches are to be found on very steep shady slopes above approximately 2500 m. Especially transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack are unfavourable. The avalanche prone locations are difficult to recognise.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
The surface of the snowpack has frozen to form a strong crust and will soften during the day. Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise to gradual softening of the snowpack below approximately 3000 m.
Steep west, north and east facing slopes: Faceted weak layers exist in the bottom section of the old snowpack at elevated altitudes.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: Outgoing longwave radiation during the night will be good. The backcountry touring conditions in the morning at elevated altitudes are generally favourable. As the day progresses as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation there will be an increase in the danger of wet avalanches.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: The danger of moist and wet avalanches will increase quickly during the day.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
The conditions are generally favourable for ski touring.
The surface of the snowpack will freeze to form a strong crust and will soften quickly.
In the late morning the natural activity of moist and wet avalanches will gradually increase. In starting zones where no previous releases have taken place and on very steep slopes more small and, in isolated cases, medium-sized moist snow slides and avalanches are possible.
Backcountry tours and ascents to alpine cabins should be concluded timely.
As a consequence of new snow and a sometimes moderate southeasterly wind, mostly small wind slabs formed on Monday. They have formed at high altitudes and in high Alpine regions.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
The surface of the snowpack has frozen to form a strong crust. New snow and wind slabs are lying on a wet old snowpack.
At higher altitudes, snow cover becomes thinner and patchy; it is better at mid-altitudes, which are less exposed to the wind. Below approximately 1900 m hardly any snow is lying.
Weak layers exist in the snowpack in particular on steep shady slopes.
As a consequence of the snow drift accumulations will stabilise during the next few days.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: As a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation more moist and wet avalanches are possible. Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise to settling of the snowpack.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: The danger of moist and wet avalanches will increase quickly during the day.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
The conditions are generally favourable for ski touring.
The surface of the snowpack will freeze to form a strong crust and will soften quickly.
In the late morning the natural activity of moist and wet avalanches will gradually increase. In starting zones where no previous releases have taken place and on very steep slopes more small and, in isolated cases, medium-sized moist snow slides and avalanches are possible.
Backcountry tours and ascents to alpine cabins should be concluded timely.
As a consequence of new snow and a sometimes moderate southeasterly wind, mostly small wind slabs formed on Monday. They have formed at high altitudes and in high Alpine regions.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
The surface of the snowpack has frozen to form a strong crust. New snow and wind slabs are lying on a wet old snowpack.
At higher altitudes, snow cover becomes thinner and patchy; it is better at mid-altitudes, which are less exposed to the wind. Below approximately 1900 m hardly any snow is lying.
Weak layers exist in the snowpack in particular on steep shady slopes.
As a consequence of the snow drift accumulations will stabilise during the next few days.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: As a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation more moist and wet avalanches are possible. Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise to settling of the snowpack.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: The weather conditions gave rise to thorough wetting of the snowpack over a wide area.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment: Wet and gliding avalanches are possible. The avalanche danger should be investigated very thoroughly in the relevant locality. The avalanche prone locations are to be found in particular at the base of rock walls, and adjacent to ridgelines and in gullies and bowls. Places where weaknesses exist in the old snowpack are especially precarious. The avalanches can be released in deep layers of the snowpack.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment: Snow cover and snowpack depth vary greatly depending on aspect and elevation; in general, the snowpack is saturated on all aspects and is becoming increasingly thin. At low and intermediate altitudes a little snow is lying on south facing slopes. Weak layers exist in the snowpack in particular on shady slopes.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: The weather will be mostly sunny.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Wet and gliding snow require caution. From starting zones where no previous releases have taken place gliding avalanches can reach intermediate altitudes.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment: In all aspects more medium-sized and, in isolated cases, large gliding avalanches and wet snow slides are possible especially in starting zones where no previous releases have taken place. Great caution and restraint are recommended.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment: The rain gave rise to a loss of strength within the snowpack.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: The danger of moist and wet avalanches will already be elevated in the early morning. In the event of an overcast night this applies.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
Early morning: The conditions are generally favourable for ski touring.
If there is cloud cover at night combined with warm air, the avalanche risk will already be 2 (moderate) by the morning because The surface of the snowpack will only just freeze.
In the late morning the natural activity of moist and wet avalanches will quickly increase. In particular in starting zones where no previous releases have taken place and on very steep slopes more medium-sized and, in isolated cases, large natural wet avalanches are to be expected.
Backcountry tours and ascents to alpine cabins should be concluded timely.
As a consequence of new snow and a sometimes moderate southeasterly wind, sometimes avalanche prone wind slabs formed on Monday. They have formed at high altitudes and in high Alpine regions.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
20 to 40 cm of snow, and even more in some localities, fell on Monday above approximately 2300 m. The weather conditions gave rise to rapid settling of the snowpack. New snow and wind slabs are lying on a wet old snowpack.
At higher altitudes, snow cover becomes thinner and patchy; it is better at mid-altitudes, which are less exposed to the wind. Below approximately 1900 m hardly any snow is lying.
Weak layers exist in the snowpack in particular on steep shady slopes.
As a consequence of the snow drift accumulations will stabilise during the next few days.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: The spring-like weather conditions will give rise to settling of the snowpack. As the moisture increases more moist and wet avalanches are possible.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: The danger of moist and wet avalanches will already be elevated in the early morning. In the event of an overcast night this applies.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
Early morning: The conditions are generally favourable for ski touring.
If there is cloud cover at night combined with warm air, the avalanche risk will already be 2 (moderate) by the morning because The surface of the snowpack will only just freeze.
In the late morning the natural activity of moist and wet avalanches will quickly increase. In particular in starting zones where no previous releases have taken place and on very steep slopes more medium-sized and, in isolated cases, large natural wet avalanches are to be expected.
Backcountry tours and ascents to alpine cabins should be concluded timely.
As a consequence of new snow and a sometimes moderate southeasterly wind, sometimes avalanche prone wind slabs formed on Monday. They have formed at high altitudes and in high Alpine regions.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
20 to 40 cm of snow, and even more in some localities, fell on Monday above approximately 2300 m. The weather conditions gave rise to rapid settling of the snowpack. New snow and wind slabs are lying on a wet old snowpack.
At higher altitudes, snow cover becomes thinner and patchy; it is better at mid-altitudes, which are less exposed to the wind. Below approximately 1900 m hardly any snow is lying.
Weak layers exist in the snowpack in particular on steep shady slopes.
As a consequence of the snow drift accumulations will stabilise during the next few days.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: The spring-like weather conditions will give rise to settling of the snowpack. As the moisture increases more moist and wet avalanches are possible.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: A clear night will be followed in the early morning by favourable conditions over a wide area, but the danger of wet avalanches will increase later.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
The avalanche danger will already increase in the late morning, especially on east, south and west facing slopes at high altitudes and in high Alpine regions, and in all aspects below approximately 2500 m.

In particular on steep sunny slopes small and medium-sized moist and wet avalanches are possible as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation. Moist avalanches can occur easily or triggered naturally. Naturally triggered avalanches and penetration of the old snow cover indicate the existence of a weak snowack.

Backcountry tours should be started early and concluded timely. Early morning: There is a danger of falling on the hard snow surface.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
Over a wide area 5 to 30 cm of snow has fallen since Sunday above approximately 2100 m.
The surface of the snowpack has frozen to form a strong crust and will already soften in the late morning. This applies in particular on steep east, south and west facing slopes below approximately 2600 m, as well as on north facing slopes below approximately 2400 m.

Below approximately 2200 m no snow is lying on south facing slopes.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: A clear night will be followed in the early morning by favourable conditions over a wide area, but the danger of wet avalanches will increase later.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
The avalanche danger will already increase in the late morning, especially on east, south and west facing slopes at high altitudes and in high Alpine regions, and in all aspects below approximately 2500 m.

In particular on steep sunny slopes small and medium-sized moist and wet avalanches are possible as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation. Moist avalanches can occur easily or triggered naturally. Naturally triggered avalanches and penetration of the old snow cover indicate the existence of a weak snowack.

Backcountry tours should be started early and concluded timely. Early morning: There is a danger of falling on the hard snow surface.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
Over a wide area 5 to 30 cm of snow has fallen since Sunday above approximately 2100 m.
The surface of the snowpack has frozen to form a strong crust and will already soften in the late morning. This applies in particular on steep east, south and west facing slopes below approximately 2600 m, as well as on north facing slopes below approximately 2400 m.

Below approximately 2200 m no snow is lying on south facing slopes.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: As a consequence of warming and solar radiation a sometimes treacherous avalanche situation will be encountered in some localities. Wet snow requires caution.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
The avalanche prone locations for wet avalanches are to be found in particular on cut slopes below approximately 2500 m. From starting zones that have retained some snow on very steep shady slopes the wet avalanches can reach large size. The new snow of Tuesday is to be evaluated with care and prudence in all aspects above approximately 2500 m. In particular on very steep slopes and at the base of rock walls and behind abrupt changes in the terrain individual small and, in isolated cases, medium-sized avalanches are possible. Bases of rock walls are especially dangerous. As a consequence of wind, sometimes avalanche prone wind slabs formed especially above approximately 2500 m. The avalanche prone locations are to be found in particular adjacent to ridgelines and in gullies and bowls in northwest to north to northeast facing aspects above approximately 2500 m. Transitions from a shallow to a deep snowpack where weaknesses exist in the old snowpack are especially dangerous.
In some localities increase in danger of moist and wet avalanches.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
Precarious weak layers exist in the snowpack on northwest, north and northeast facing slopes.
Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise as the day progresses to a loss of strength within the snowpack over a wide area in particular on steep slopes below approximately 2700 m. The new snow of Tuesday is loosely bonded above approximately 2800 m. The wind slabs are poorly bonded with the old snowpack above approximately 2700 m.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights:
The weather will be partly cloudy over a wide area.
Warming.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
The avalanche prone locations for dry avalanches are to be found on steep shady slopes above approximately 2500 m.
As a consequence of warming during the day and the solar radiation, the likelihood of wet avalanches being released will increase in particular on steep shady slopes below approximately 2500 m.
Medium-sized avalanches are possible.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
The surface of the snowpack will already soften in the late morning.
Above approximately 2400 m snow fell in the last few days in some regions.
As a consequence of wind, mostly shallow wind slabs formed in high Alpine regions.
Steep shady slopes above approximately 2500 m: Faceted weak layers exist in the bottom section of the old snowpack.
Below approximately 2000 m from a snow sport perspective, in most cases insufficient snow is lying.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Wet snow requires caution.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment: Only isolated wet avalanches are possible. Caution is to be exercised on extremely steep shady slopes. In very isolated cases avalanches are medium-sized.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment: The surface of the snowpack will freeze to form a strong crust and will soften during the day.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: Low avalanche danger will prevail. Wet snow requires caution.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Wet snow requires caution.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment: Only isolated wet avalanches are possible. Caution is to be exercised on extremely steep shady slopes. In very isolated cases avalanches are medium-sized.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment: The surface of the snowpack will freeze to form a strong crust and will soften during the day.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: Low avalanche danger will prevail. Wet snow requires caution.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights:
The weather will be partly cloudy over a wide area.
Warming.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
The surface of the snowpack will only just freeze and will already be soft in the early morning.
In isolated cases the avalanches are small.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment: As a consequence of mild temperatures the snowpack consolidated. As a consequence of warming during the day there will be an increase in the danger of moist and wet avalanches.
